concepts.title: Linear and Non-Linear Differential Equations
concepts.conceptBody:
Linear and non-linear differential equation

A differential equation which is of degree one in the dependent variable (unknown variable) and its derivative is called a linear differential equation.

A differential equation which is not linear is called a non-linear differential equation.

A differential equation is linear if it can be expressed as

${{a}_{0}}\frac{{{d}^{n}}y}{d{{x}^{n}}}+{{a}_{1}}\frac{{{d}^{n-1}}y}{d{{x}^{n-1}}}+\ldots +{{a}_{n-1}}\frac{dy}{dx}+{{a}_{n}}y={{P}_{n}}$

Where ${{a}_{0}},{{a}_{1}},{{a}_{2}},\ldots ,{{a}_{n}}and\,{{p}_{n}}$ are constants or functions of independent variable x.

Thus a differential equation will be non-linear differential equation if and only if

  1. its degree is more than one
  2. exponent of the dependent variable is more than one.
  3. any of the differential coefficient has exponent more than one
  4. products containing dependent variable and its differential coefficients are present.

Examples : 

1) $2\frac{{{\text{d}}^{3}}\text{y}}{\text{d}{{\text{x}}^{3}}}-5{{\left( \frac{\text{dy}}{\text{dx}} \right)}^{2}}+7\text{y}={{\text{x}}^{2}}\,\,(non-linear)$ 

$Here\,exponent\,of\,\,\frac{dy}{dx}\,\,is\,\,2.$ 

2) $y\frac{dy}{dx}=x\quad (non-linear)$

$Here\,degree\,of\,the\,term\,y\frac{dy}{dx}\,\,is\,\,2.$

concepts.title: Understanding of the First Order Differential Equation
concepts.conceptBody:
First Order Differential Equation

A first order differential equation is a relation

$\frac{dy}{dx}=f(x,y)$

in which $f(x,y)$ is a function of two variables defined on a region in the xy-plane.

Example : 

The equation $\frac{dy}{dx}=1-\frac{y}{x}$ is a first order differential equation in which $f(x,y)=1-\frac{y}{x}$.

concepts.title: Solution of Linear Differential Equation done with the help of I.F
concepts.conceptBody:
Linear Differential Equation 

A differential equation is linear if the dependent variable (y) and its derivative appear only in the first degree. The general form of a linear differential equation of first degree is

$\frac{dy}{dx}+Py=Q$

Where $\text{P}$ and $\text{Q}$ are the functions of $\text{x}$ (& not of $\text{y}$ ).

Example : $\frac{dy}{dx}+xy={{x}^{2}},\frac{xdy}{dx}+2y={{x}^{3}},\frac{dy}{dx}+2y=\text{sin}x$

Similarly, $\frac{dx}{dy}+Px=Q$ is a linear differential equation, where P and Q are functions of y$(\And \text{not}$ of x).

Solution of Linear differential equation.

Multiplying equation (i) throughout by ${{\text{e}}^{\int \text{Pdx}}}$, we get

$\begin{align} & \frac{dy}{dx}\cdot {{e}^{\int{P}dx}}+Py\cdot {{e}^{\int{P}dx}}=Q\cdot {{e}^{\int{P}dx}} \\ & or\frac{d}{dx}\left( y\cdot {{e}^{\int{P}dx}} \right)=Q\cdot {{e}^{\int{P}dx}} \\ \end{align}$

Integrating both sides w.r.t. $\text{x}$, we get

$y\cdot {{e}^{\int ~Pdx}}=\int ~Q\cdot {{e}^{\int ~Pdx}}dx+c$

Which is the required solution.

The factor ${{e}^{\int Pdx}}$ is called an integration factor of the differential equation & is shortly written as I.F. 

Remember: The solution of $\frac{dy}{dx}+Pt=Q$ is $y\cdot \left( I.F \right)=\int Q\cdot \left( I.F \right)dx+c$.

concepts.title: Steps to check whether the given Differential equation can be reduced to Linear form or not
concepts.conceptBody:
How to decide whether given D.E. is linear or not 

Step-1 : Make R.H.S. independent of y.

Step-2 : Make coefficeint of $\frac{dy}{dx}$ independent of x

Step-3 : If derivative of terms of y from middle term $=$ coefficeint of $\frac{dy}{dx}$ term then the equation is reducible to linear D.E. by assuming the coefficient of middle term $=\text{t}$ else interchange middle term & R.H.S. in original D.E. & then go to Step-1 again.

For example 

take D.E., $\frac{\text{dy}}{\text{dx}}+\frac{\text{y}}{\text{x}}\text{log}\left( \text{y} \right)=\frac{\text{y}{{(\text{logy})}^{2}}}{{{\text{x}}^{2}}}$

Step-1 $\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ R.H.S. independent of y

$\frac{1}{y{{(\text{log}y)}^{2}}}\frac{dy}{dx}+\frac{1}{\left( \text{log}y \right)x}=\frac{1}{{{x}^{2}}}$

Step-2 : Coefficient of $\frac{dy}{dx}$ independent of x

Step-3 : $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Middle term $=\frac{1}{\text{xlogy}}$

& terms of $y=\frac{1}{\left( \text{log }y \right)}$

derivative of terms of $y=\frac{d}{dx}\left( \frac{1}{\text{log}y} \right)=-\frac{1}{y{{(\text{log}y)}^{2}}}=$ coefficient of $\frac{dy}{dx}$

Hence this is in Linear form

$\begin{align} & \text{now put }\quad \frac{1}{\log y}=t \\ & -\frac{1}{y{{(\log y)}^{2}}}dy=dt \\ \end{align}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ original $\text{DE}$ becomes

$-\frac{dt}{dx}+\frac{t}{x}=\frac{1}{{{x}^{2}}}$

  • $\frac{\text{dt}}{\text{dx}}-\frac{\text{t}}{\text{x}}=-\frac{1}{{{\text{x}}^{2}}}$

which is our normal linear D.E. Solve it using the method used in earlier questions.

(2) But now take the example

$\frac{dy}{dx}-\left( 1+x \right){{e}^{x}}\text{sec}y=\frac{\text{tan }y}{1+x}$

applying the above procedure here too

$\frac{1}{\text{tan }y}\left( \frac{dy}{dx} \right)-\frac{\left( 1+x \right){{e}^{x}}}{\text{tan }y}\text{sec }y=\frac{1}{\left( 1+x \right)}$

now terms of y in middle terms $=\frac{\text{sec}y}{\text{tan}y}=\text{cosec y}$

& \(\frac{d}{dy}\left( \text{cos}ec\,y \right)\ne \frac{1}{\text{tan}\,y}\)

as it is - cotx cosecy, hence not in linear from

so going through the else part, i.e. interchanging the middle term and R.H.S.

so DE becomes

$\frac{dy}{dx}-\frac{\text{tan }y}{1+x}=\left( 1+x \right){{e}^{x}}\text{sec }y$

removing terms of y from R.H.S.

$\begin{align} & \frac{1}{\sec y}\frac{dy}{dx}-\frac{\tan y}{\sec y(1+x)}=(1+x){{e}^{x}} \\ & \cos y\,\,\frac{dy}{dx}-\frac{\sin y}{(1+x)}=(1+x){{e}^{x}}\text{ } \\ & \text{now }\frac{d}{dx}(\sin y)=-\cos y \\ \end{align}$

which is equal to coefficeint of $\frac{dy}{dx}$ except for the minus sign which can neglected.

concepts.title: Bernoulli's Equation
concepts.conceptBody:
Bernoulli's Equation

For this type, the equation is as follows

$\frac{dy}{dx}+Py=Q{{y}^{n}}$

where $\text{P}\And \text{Q}$ are functions of $\text{x}$ alone and do not contain $\text{y}$. How to solve it then :

Step-1 : Make R.H.S. free of y

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ dividing by ${{\text{y}}^{\text{n}}}$

$\frac{1}{{{y}^{n}}}\frac{dy}{dx}+\frac{P}{{{y}^{n-1}}}=Q$

Step-2 : Make the coefficient of $\frac{dy}{dx}$ free of x. Here it is already free so go to Step-3.

Step-3 : Substitute $\frac{1}{{{y}^{n-1}}}=t\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ {Substituting the coefficient of middle term i.e.f.}

$\frac{-\left( \text{n}-1 \right)}{{{\text{y}}^{\text{n}}}}\frac{\text{dy}}{\text{dx}}=\frac{\text{dt}}{\text{dx}}$

putting in DE

$\begin{matrix} \frac{1}{\left( 1-n \right)}\frac{dt}{dx}+P=Q \\ \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{dt}{dx}+P\left( 1-n \right)=Q\left( 1-n \right) \\ \end{matrix}$

which becomes our normal linear form now & can be solved as done earlier.

Q) (a) $\left( y\,\text{log}\,x-1 \right)ydx=xdy$ (b) $\text{se}{{\text{c}}^{2}}y\frac{dy}{dx}+x\,\text{tan}\,y={{x}^{3}}$ 

Solution. 

  1. $\left( y\text{log}x-1 \right)ydx=xdy$

$\begin{align} & {{y}^{2}}\log x-y=x\frac{dy}{dx}\text{ } \\ & \text{or }\quad \,\,\,x\frac{dy}{dx}+y={{y}^{2}}\log x \\ \end{align}$

or $\frac{1}{{{y}^{2}}}\frac{dy}{dx}+\frac{1}{xy}=\frac{\text{log}x}{x}$

Now put $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{1}{y}=\text{t}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }-\frac{1}{{{y}^{2}}}\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{dt}{dx}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }-\frac{dt}{dx}+\frac{t}{x}=\frac{\text{log}x}{x}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{dt}{dx}-\frac{t}{x}=\frac{-\text{log}x}{x}$

Now this is normal linear form

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ I}\text{.F}\text{. }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }={{e}^{\int ~P\cdot dx}}={{e}^{\int ~-\frac{1}{x}dx}}={{e}^{-\text{log}x}}={{e}^{\text{log}\left( 1/x \right)}}=\frac{1}{x}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Solution becomes

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ t}\text{. }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( \frac{1}{x} \right)=\int ~-\left( \frac{\text{log}x}{x} \right)\left( \frac{1}{x} \right)dx=\int ~-\left( \frac{\text{log}x}{{{x}^{2}}} \right)dx$

integratign using by parts

$\begin{align} & \frac{\text{t}}{\text{x}}=\frac{1}{\text{x}}\log \text{x}-\int{\frac{1}{\text{x}}}\cdot \frac{1}{\text{x}}\text{dx } \\ & \text{or }\quad \frac{\text{t}}{\text{x}}=\frac{1}{\text{x}}\log \text{x}+\frac{1}{\text{x}}+\text{C} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \text{t}=\log \text{x}+1+\text{Cx} \\ & =\log \text{x}+\log \text{e}+\text{Cx} \\ & =\log \text{ex}+\text{Cx} \\ \end{align}$

replacing the value of t

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{1}{y}=\text{logex}+\text{Cx}$

  1. $\text{se}{{\text{c}}^{2}}y\frac{dy}{dx}+x\,\text{tan}\,y={{x}^{3}}$

This is again bernoulli's equation through simplified one as step-1 & step-2 have already been done here. Only step-3 is required to be done

putting tan y = t

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \Rightarrow & \text{se}{{\text{c}}^{2}}y\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{dt}{dx} \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{dt}{dx}+xt={{x}^{3}} \\ \end{array}$

Now this is normal Linear form

$\begin{align} & \therefore \text{ }\text{I}\text{.F}\text{. }={{\text{e}}^{\int{x}~\text{d}x}}={{\text{e}}^{{{\text{x}}^{3}}/2}}\,\,\text{ } \\ & \text{t}\text{. }{{\text{e}}^{{{\text{x}}^{2}}/2}}=\int{{{\text{x}}^{3}}}{{\text{e}}^{{{\text{x}}^{2}}/2}} \\ \end{align}$

for R.H.S. now putting $\frac{{{x}^{2}}}{2}=z$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \Rightarrow & \text{xdx}=\text{dz} \\ \Rightarrow & \text{t}\cdot {{\text{e}}^{{{\text{x}}^{2}}/2}}=\int ~{{2}^{\text{z}}}{{\text{e}}^{\text{z}}}\text{dt} \\ \end{array}$

using by parts

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ t}\text{. }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{e}}^{{{\text{x}}^{2}}/2}}=2{{\text{e}}^{\text{z}}}\left( \text{z}-1 \right)+\text{C}$

replacing value of $\text{t}\And \text{z}$

$\begin{align} & \Rightarrow \tan \text{y}\cdot {{\text{e}}^{{{\text{x}}^{2}}/2}}=2{{\text{e}}^{{{\text{x}}^{2}}/2}}\left( \frac{{{\text{x}}^{2}}}{2}-1 \right)+\text{C} \\ & \Rightarrow \tan \text{y}=2\left( \frac{{{\text{x}}^{2}}}{2}-1 \right)+\text{C}{{\text{e}}^{-{{\text{x}}^{2}}/2}} \\ & \therefore solution\,\,is\,\, \\ & \tan y=\left( {{x}^{2}}-2 \right)+\text{C}{{\text{e}}^{-2{{x}^{2}}/2}} \\ \end{align}$
